---
engine: julia
---

## Nonstationarity in Extreme Value Analysis ✏️ {.unnumbered}

This notebook demonstrates how to model and compare stationary and nonstationary extreme value models.
We explore the trade-offs between bias (from ignoring trends) and increased uncertainty (from adding parameters) that characterize the nonstationarity dilemma in extreme value analysis.

```{julia}
using DataFrames
using Statistics
using Random
using Distributions
using CairoMakie
using LaTeXStrings
using Optim

CairoMakie.activate!(; type="svg")
```

## Synthetic data with known trend

We start with synthetic data where we can control the degree of nonstationarity.
This allows us to evaluate how well different models recover the true underlying process.

```{julia}
#| label: synthetic-setup

# Generate synthetic annual maximum data with a linear trend
function generate_nonstationary_data(n_years; μ₀=100, σ=20, ξ=0.05, trend=0.5)
    Random.seed!(42)
    years = 1:n_years

    # Location parameter increases linearly with time
    μ_t = μ₀ .+ trend .* (years .- 1)

    # Generate GEV-distributed annual maxima
    data = [rand(GeneralizedExtremeValue(μ_t[i], σ, ξ)) for i in 1:n_years]

    return DataFrame(
        year = years,
        annual_max = data,
        true_location = μ_t
    ), (μ₀=μ₀, σ=σ, ξ=ξ, trend=trend)
end

# Generate 50 years of data with a moderate trend
n_years = 50
synthetic_data, true_params = generate_nonstationary_data(n_years; trend=0.8)

println("Generated $n_years years of synthetic data")
println("True parameters: μ₀=$(true_params.μ₀), σ=$(true_params.σ), ξ=$(true_params.ξ), trend=$(true_params.trend)")
first(synthetic_data, 5)
```

```{julia}
#| label: plot-synthetic-data
#| fig-cap: "Synthetic annual maximum data with known linear trend. The true underlying location parameter (red line) increases linearly with time."

fig = Figure(size=(800, 500))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
    title="Synthetic Annual Maxima with Linear Trend",
    xlabel="Year",
    ylabel="Annual Maximum Value")

# Plot the synthetic observations
scatter!(ax, synthetic_data.year, synthetic_data.annual_max,
    color=:steelblue, alpha=0.7, label="Observations")

# Plot the true location parameter trend
lines!(ax, synthetic_data.year, synthetic_data.true_location,
    color=:red, linewidth=3, label="True location μ(t)")

axislegend(ax, position=:lt)
fig
```

## Maximum likelihood estimation

We'll use maximum likelihood estimation to fit both stationary and nonstationary models, which is more numerically stable than the Bayesian approach for this demonstration.

```{julia}
#| label: mle-functions

# Negative log-likelihood for stationary GEV
function stationary_gev_nll(params, data)
    μ, σ, ξ = params

    if σ <= 0
        return Inf
    end

    nll = 0.0
    for x in data
        z = (x - μ) / σ
        if 1 + ξ * z <= 0
            return Inf
        end

        if abs(ξ) < 1e-6  # Gumbel case
            nll += log(σ) + z + exp(-z)
        else  # General GEV case
            nll += log(σ) + (1 + 1/ξ) * log(1 + ξ * z) + (1 + ξ * z)^(-1/ξ)
        end
    end

    return nll
end

# Negative log-likelihood for nonstationary GEV with linear trend
function nonstationary_gev_nll(params, data, years)
    μ₀, μ₁, σ, ξ = params

    if σ <= 0
        return Inf
    end

    nll = 0.0
    for (i, x) in enumerate(data)
        μ_t = μ₀ + μ₁ * (years[i] - 1)
        z = (x - μ_t) / σ

        if 1 + ξ * z <= 0
            return Inf
        end

        if abs(ξ) < 1e-6  # Gumbel case
            nll += log(σ) + z + exp(-z)
        else  # General GEV case
            nll += log(σ) + (1 + 1/ξ) * log(1 + ξ * z) + (1 + ξ * z)^(-1/ξ)
        end
    end

    return nll
end

println("Defined likelihood functions")
```

```{julia}
#| label: fit-models

# Fit stationary model
stationary_init = [mean(synthetic_data.annual_max), std(synthetic_data.annual_max), 0.1]
stationary_result = optimize(p -> stationary_gev_nll(p, synthetic_data.annual_max),
                           stationary_init)
stationary_params = Optim.minimizer(stationary_result)

println("Stationary model parameters:")
println("μ = $(round(stationary_params[1], digits=2))")
println("σ = $(round(stationary_params[2], digits=2))")
println("ξ = $(round(stationary_params[3], digits=3))")

# Fit nonstationary model
nonstationary_init = [mean(synthetic_data.annual_max), 0.5, std(synthetic_data.annual_max), 0.1]
nonstationary_result = optimize(p -> nonstationary_gev_nll(p, synthetic_data.annual_max, synthetic_data.year),
                              nonstationary_init)
nonstationary_params = Optim.minimizer(nonstationary_result)

println("\nNonstationary model parameters:")
println("μ₀ = $(round(nonstationary_params[1], digits=2))")
println("μ₁ = $(round(nonstationary_params[2], digits=3)) (true: $(true_params.trend))")
println("σ = $(round(nonstationary_params[3], digits=2))")
println("ξ = $(round(nonstationary_params[4], digits=3))")
```

```{julia}
#| label: model-comparison
#| fig-cap: "Comparison of stationary and nonstationary model fits. The nonstationary model correctly captures the underlying trend, while the stationary model averages over the time period."

fig = Figure(size=(800, 600))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
    title="Model Comparison: Stationary vs. Nonstationary",
    xlabel="Year",
    ylabel="Location Parameter μ")

# Plot true trend
lines!(ax, synthetic_data.year, synthetic_data.true_location,
    color=:red, linewidth=3, label="True μ(t)")

# Plot stationary model estimate (constant over time)
μ_stationary = stationary_params[1]
hlines!(ax, [μ_stationary], color=:green, linewidth=3,
    linestyle=:dash, label="Stationary estimate")

# Plot nonstationary model trend
μ₀_est, μ₁_est = nonstationary_params[1], nonstationary_params[2]
trend_est = μ₀_est .+ μ₁_est .* (synthetic_data.year .- 1)
lines!(ax, synthetic_data.year, trend_est,
    color=:blue, linewidth=3, label="Nonstationary estimate")

axislegend(ax, position=:lt)
fig
```

## Return level comparison

The key practical question is how nonstationarity affects return level estimates:

```{julia}
#| label: return-level-comparison
#| fig-cap: "Return level comparison between stationary and nonstationary models. The nonstationary model provides time-specific estimates that vary with the underlying trend."

# Function to calculate return levels for GEV distribution
function gev_return_level(μ, σ, ξ, return_period)
    p = 1 - 1/return_period
    if abs(ξ) < 1e-6
        # Gumbel case (ξ ≈ 0)
        return μ - σ * log(-log(p))
    else
        # General GEV case
        return μ + (σ/ξ) * ((-log(p))^(-ξ) - 1)
    end
end

# Calculate 100-year return levels for different time points
return_period = 100
future_years = [2024, 2050, 2100]  # Assuming synthetic data starts at year 1 = 2024

fig = Figure(size=(800, 600))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
    title="100-Year Return Levels: Impact of Nonstationarity",
    xlabel="Year",
    ylabel="100-Year Return Level")

# Stationary model return level (constant over time)
σ_stat, ξ_stat = stationary_params[2], stationary_params[3]
rl_stationary = gev_return_level(μ_stationary, σ_stat, ξ_stat, return_period)
hlines!(ax, [rl_stationary], color=:green, linewidth=3,
    linestyle=:dash, label="Stationary")

# Nonstationary model return levels
σ_nonstat, ξ_nonstat = nonstationary_params[3], nonstationary_params[4]

years_extended = 2024:(2024+n_years+76)  # Extend to 2100
μ_extended = μ₀_est .+ μ₁_est .* (years_extended .- 2024)
rl_nonstationary = [gev_return_level(μ, σ_nonstat, ξ_nonstat, return_period) for μ in μ_extended]

lines!(ax, years_extended, rl_nonstationary,
    color=:blue, linewidth=3, label="Nonstationary")

# Mark specific years
for year in future_years
    idx = year - 2024 + 1
    if idx <= length(rl_nonstationary)
        scatter!(ax, [year], [rl_nonstationary[idx]],
            color=:red, markersize=10)
        text!(ax, year, rl_nonstationary[idx] + 5,
            text="$(round(rl_nonstationary[idx], digits=1))")
    end
end

axislegend(ax, position=:lt)
fig
```

## Model selection using likelihood ratio test

We can use statistical tests to evaluate whether the trend is statistically significant:

```{julia}
#| label: model-selection

# Likelihood ratio test
stationary_loglik = -Optim.minimum(stationary_result)
nonstationary_loglik = -Optim.minimum(nonstationary_result)

# Test statistic (follows chi-squared distribution with 1 df)
lr_statistic = 2 * (nonstationary_loglik - stationary_loglik)
p_value = 1 - cdf(Chisq(1), lr_statistic)

println("Model Selection Results:")
println("Stationary log-likelihood: $(round(stationary_loglik, digits=2))")
println("Nonstationary log-likelihood: $(round(nonstationary_loglik, digits=2))")
println("Likelihood ratio statistic: $(round(lr_statistic, digits=2))")
println("P-value: $(round(p_value, digits=4))")

if p_value < 0.05
    println("→ Nonstationary model is significantly better (p < 0.05)")
else
    println("→ No significant evidence for nonstationarity (p ≥ 0.05)")
end

# AIC comparison
aic_stationary = 2 * 3 - 2 * stationary_loglik  # 3 parameters
aic_nonstationary = 2 * 4 - 2 * nonstationary_loglik  # 4 parameters

println("\nAIC Comparison:")
println("Stationary AIC: $(round(aic_stationary, digits=1))")
println("Nonstationary AIC: $(round(aic_nonstationary, digits=1))")
println("ΔAIC: $(round(aic_nonstationary - aic_stationary, digits=1))")

if aic_nonstationary < aic_stationary
    println("→ Nonstationary model preferred by AIC")
else
    println("→ Stationary model preferred by AIC")
end
```

## Uncertainty visualization

Even with point estimates, we can explore how parameter uncertainty might affect our conclusions:

```{julia}
#| label: uncertainty-sensitivity
#| fig-cap: "Sensitivity of 100-year return levels to the trend parameter. Small uncertainties in trend estimation can lead to large differences in projected return levels."

# Explore sensitivity to trend parameter
trend_range = (μ₁_est - 0.5):0.1:(μ₁_est + 0.5)
future_year = 2100
year_offset = future_year - 2024

fig = Figure(size=(800, 500))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
    title="Sensitivity of 2100 Return Level to Trend Parameter",
    xlabel="Trend μ₁ (mm/year)",
    ylabel="100-Year Return Level in 2100 (mm)")

# Calculate return levels for different trend values
rl_sensitivity = []
for trend in trend_range
    μ_2100 = μ₀_est + trend * year_offset
    rl = gev_return_level(μ_2100, σ_nonstat, ξ_nonstat, return_period)
    push!(rl_sensitivity, rl)
end

lines!(ax, collect(trend_range), rl_sensitivity,
    color=:navy, linewidth=3)

# Mark the estimated trend
scatter!(ax, [μ₁_est], [gev_return_level(μ₀_est + μ₁_est * year_offset, σ_nonstat, ξ_nonstat, return_period)],
    color=:red, markersize=12, label="MLE estimate")

# Mark true trend
true_rl_2100 = gev_return_level(true_params.μ₀ + true_params.trend * year_offset, true_params.σ, true_params.ξ, return_period)
scatter!(ax, [true_params.trend], [true_rl_2100],
    color=:green, markersize=12, label="True value")

axislegend(ax, position=:lt)
fig
```

## Practical considerations

This analysis demonstrates several key points about nonstationarity in extreme value analysis:

### 1. Model selection uncertainty

Even when a trend exists, the decision to include it depends on:
- Strength of the trend relative to natural variability
- Length of the data record
- Purpose of the analysis (short-term vs. long-term planning)

### 2. Uncertainty propagation

Nonstationary models typically have larger parameter uncertainty, which propagates to return level estimates.
The trade-off between bias (ignoring trends) and variance (estimating additional parameters) depends on the specific application.

### 3. Physical plausibility

Model selection should consider physical understanding of the climate system.
Linear trends may be appropriate for temperature-related variables under climate change, but precipitation extremes may exhibit more complex patterns.

This notebook demonstrates the practical challenges of incorporating nonstationarity in extreme value analysis.
The choice between stationary and nonstationary models should be guided by physical understanding, statistical evidence, and the intended use of the results.
